elements.sent-2411-001: AI-driven models have demonstrated significant potential in automating radiology report generation for chest X-rays.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, there is no standardized benchmark for objectively evaluating their performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this, we present ReXrank (\href{https://rexrank.ai{https://rexrank.ai ), a public leaderboard and challenge for assessing AI-powered radiology report generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Our framework incorporates ReXGradient, the largest test dataset consisting of 10,000 studies, and three public datasets (MIMIC-CXR, IU-Xray, CheXpert Plus) for report generation assessment.
elements.sent-2411-005: ReXrank employs 8 evaluation metrics and separately assesses models capable of generating only findings sections and those providing both findings and impressions sections.
elements.sent-2411-006: By providing this standardized evaluation framework, ReXrank enables meaningful comparisons of model performance and offers crucial insights into their robustness across diverse clinical settings.
elements.sent-2411-007: Beyond its current focus on chest X-rays, ReXrank's framework sets the stage for comprehensive evaluation of automated reporting across the full spectrum of medical imaging.
elements.sent-2411-008: Writing accurate radiology reports from medical images is a critical but complex task, requiring both deep expertise in medical imaging and the ability to accurately interpret and articulate intricate findings.
elements.sent-2411-009: The demand for such reports has surged with the rapid advancements in imaging technologies, leading to increased workloads for radiologists, risks of information loss, and longer report turnaround times~\cite{bruls2020workload .}
elements.sent-2411-010: AI-driven solutions have emerged as a potential answer to these challenges, serving as assistive tools to enhance reporting efficiency and ensure access to high-quality, specialty-level interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Medical visual-language models have shown promise in automating the generation of radiology reports from chest X-ray images~\cite{chen2024chexagent,pellegrini2023radialog,chen2023cross,lee2023llm,zhou2024generalist .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, as the field of AI-assisted medical reporting rapidly evolves, there is a growing need for standardized benchmarks to objectively assess and compare the performance of these models.
elements.sent-2411-013: Existing datasets for chest X-ray report generation, such as MIMIC-CXR~\cite{johnson2019mimic , are valuable but exhibit limitations that hinder their effectiveness for benchmarking.}
elements.sent-2411-014: These datasets frequently suffer from inconsistent data splits and a lack of standardized metrics during evaluation, which impedes reliable comparative analysis across different model architectures.
elements.sent-2411-015: Furthermore, the data distribution in MIMIC-CXR, commonly used in model training, fails to adequately test the models' ability to generalize to new, unseen distributions.
elements.sent-2411-016: To fill this gap, we introduce ReXrank~(\href{https://rexrank.ai{https://rexrank.ai ), a public leaderboard and challenge specifically designed for evaluating AI-powered radiology report generation from chest X-ray images.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: ReXrank offers a comprehensive evaluation framework that sets a standardized benchmark for assessing the effectiveness of different radiology report generation models.
elements.sent-2411-018: To ensure robust and clinically relevant evaluations, it integrates diverse datasets, including MIMIC-CXR~\cite{johnson2019mimic , IU-Xray~\cite{demner2016preparing , CheXpert Plus~\cite{chambon2024chexpert , and ReXGradient, a large-scale private dataset of 10,000 studies.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: This broad dataset spectrum allows us to evaluate model performance on data with varying distributions, providing deeper insights into the models' generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, ReXrank implements various report evaluation metrics, including BLEU-2~\cite{papineni2002bleu , BERTScore~\cite{zhang2019bertscore , SembScore~\cite{smit2020chexbert , RadGraph-F1~\cite{yu2023evaluating , RadCliQ~\cite{yu2023evaluating , RaTEScore~\cite{zhao2024ratescore , GREEN~\cite{ostmeier2024green , FineRadScore~\cite{huang2024fineradscore , etc., to offer a detailed view of each model's strengths and weaknesses.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This comprehensive approach enables a more nuanced understanding of model performance and facilitates meaningful comparisons between different AI-powered radiology report generation systems.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/framework.pdf} \caption{An illustration of ReXrank, a public leaderboard and challenge for AI-powered radiology report generation from chest X-ray images. ReXrank supports model submissions and evaluates them on both public datasets and a large-scale private dataset, providing comprehensive rankings of all submitted models.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-022: ReXrank leverages three public datasets and one comprehensive private dataset for report generation assessment.
elements.sent-2411-023: The private ReXGradient dataset comprises 10,000 studies from 67 U.S.
elements.sent-2411-024: For public datasets, we utilize the official test splits of MIMIC-CXR (2,347 studies) and IU-Xray (590 studies), along with CheXpert Plus's validation set (200 studies) as no test split is available.. Models.
elements.sent-2411-025: ReXrank currently includes 16 report generation models from 10 different institutions, including BiomedGPT\_IU~\cite{zhang2024generalist , CheXagent~\cite{chen2024chexagent , CheXpertPlus\_CheX~\cite{chambon2024chexpert , CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC~\cite{chambon2024chexpert , CheXpertPlus\_MIMIC~\cite{chambon2024chexpert , Cvt2distilgpt2\_IU~\cite{nicolson2023improving , Cvt2distilgpt2\_MIMIC~\cite{nicolson2023improving , GPT4V~\cite{yang2023dawn , LLM-CXR~\cite{lee2023llm , MAIRA-2~\cite{bannur2024maira , MedVersa~\cite{zhou2024generalist , RadFM~\cite{wu2023towards , RaDialog~\cite{pellegrini2023radialog , RGRG~\cite{tanida2023interactive , VLCI\_IU~\cite{chen2023cross and VLCI\_MIMIC~\cite{chen2023cross .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: These models were trained on different medical datasets, primarily MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert Plus, and IU-Xray, with some models capable of handling multiple tasks beyond just report generation.. Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-027: ReXrank employs 8 different metrics to comprehensively assess the quality of generated radiology reports, including traditional text generation metrics like BLEU-2~\cite{papineni2002bleu and BERTScore~\cite{zhang2019bertscore , as well as domain-specific metrics designed for radiology report evaluation such as SembScore~\cite{smit2020chexbert , RadGraph-F1~\cite{yu2023evaluating , RadCliQ-v1~\cite{yu2023evaluating and RaTEScore~\cite{zhao2024ratescore .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The framework also incorporates recently developed LLM-based metrics including GREEN~\cite{ostmeier2024green , and FineRadScore~\cite{huang2024fineradscore , which focus on identifying clinically significant errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Each metric evaluates different aspects of the generated reports, from textual similarity to clinical accuracy, providing a thorough assessment of model performance.
elements.sent-2411-030: We default use RadCliQ-v1 as the primary metric.. Results.
elements.sent-2411-031: MedVersa emerges as one of the top-performing models (Figure~\ref{fig:model_ranking ), with best 1/RadCliQ-v1 scores of 0.98 짹 0.05 on ReXGradient and 0.92 짹 0.02 on MIMIC-CXR.}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, its performance on the CheXpert Plus dataset is comparatively lower, ranking fourth with a 1/RadCliQ-v1 score of 0.72 짹 0.10 on the Findings.
elements.sent-2411-033: MedVersa consistently outperforms GPT4V, the state-of-the-art generalist vision-language model, across multiple metrics and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-034: We further analyze the distribution of evaluation metrics across datasets.
elements.sent-2411-035: Among the four datasets, IU X-ray stands out as the least challenging, consistently yielding high performance across models.
elements.sent-2411-036: In contrast, CheXpert Plus exhibits the highest variance and lower overall performance, likely due to its distinct data distribution and small validation set (200 studies).
elements.sent-2411-037: The private ReXGradient dataset demonstrates remarkably low-performance variance across models, underscoring its high data quality and utility as a benchmark for assessing model robustness.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/average_metrics_barchart.png} \caption{Comprehensive performance evaluation and ranking of report generation models based on the average 1/RadCliQ-v1 metric of four distinct datasets: ReXGradient, MIMIC-CXR, IU X-ray, and CheXpert Plus. MedVersa (highlighted in purple) demonstrates consistently superior performance, achieving significantly higher scores compared to other models, including GPT4V (highlighted in yellow).} \label{fig:model_ranking}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/metrics_distribution_boxplot.pdf} \caption{Distribution of evaluation metrics across different datasets: ReXGradient, and MIMIC-CXR, IU X-ray, CheXpert Plus. Box plots show the variation in model performance for each metric. For consistency in visualization, we plot the reciprocals (1/x) of FineRadScore and RadCliQ-v1, so higher values indicate better performance across all metrics.} \label{fig:metric_dataset}
elements.sent-2411-038: Our evaluation leverages four distinct datasets: ReXGradient, MIMIC-CXR, IU-Xray, and CheXpert Plus.
elements.sent-2411-039: These datasets provide diverse testing distributions across different medical institutions and patient populations.. ReXGradient.
elements.sent-2411-040: This private test set is provided by Gradient Health, which consists of 10,000 studies collected from 7,004 patients across 67 medical sites in the United States.. MIMIC-CXR~\cite{johnson2019mimic.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This is a large, publicly accessible dataset comprising 377,110 chest X-rays (CXRs) corresponding to 227,835 radiographic studies performed at the Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Boston, MA.
elements.sent-2411-042: For this dataset, we extracted sections of indication, comparison, findings, and impression via keyword matching.
elements.sent-2411-043: In our experiments, we follow MIMIC-CXR's official split and report scores on the test set, which consists of 2,347 studies.. IU-Xray ~\cite{demner2016preparing.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This is a publicly accessible dataset containing 7,470 pairs of CXRs and radiology reports.
elements.sent-2411-045: Each study in this dataset includes one frontal and one lateral CXR, associated with a single radiology report.
elements.sent-2411-046: We follow the split provided by R2Gen \cite{chen2020generating and report scores on the test set, which comprises 590 studies.. CheXpert Plus~\cite{chambon2024chexpert.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This is a large, publicly accessible consisting of 223,462 unique pairs of radiology reports and chest X-rays.
elements.sent-2411-048: These correspond to 187,711 radiographic studies from 64,725 patients.
elements.sent-2411-049: We follow the official split of CheXpert Plus and report scores on the validation set, which contains 200 studies.
elements.sent-2411-050: For each study in our test datasets, the data is organized in a structured format.
elements.sent-2411-051: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) is a widely used metric in machine translation and text generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-052: It evaluates the quality of generated text by comparing n-gram precision between the candidate and reference texts, with scores ranging from 0 to 1.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this work, we specifically use BLEU-2, which focuses on bigram precision to assess the quality of the generated text.. BERTScore~\cite{zhang2019bertscore.}
elements.sent-2411-054: BERTScore is a neural metric that uses pre-trained BERT models~\cite{kenton2019bert to evaluate text similarity.}
elements.sent-2411-055: It computes cosine similarity between the BERT embeddings of model-generated and groundtruth radiology reports.. SembScore~\cite{smit2020chexbert.}
elements.sent-2411-056: SembScore (CheXbert labeler vector similarity) is a domain-specific metric for radiology report evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-057: It computes the cosine similarity between the indicator vectors of 14 pathologies that the CheXbert automatic labeler extracts from model-generated and groundtruth radiology reports.. RadGraph-F1~\cite{yu2023evaluating.}
elements.sent-2411-058: RadGraph-F1 is a metric for radiology report evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-059: It computes the overlap in clinical entities and relations that RadGraph~\cite{jain2021radgraph extracts from candidate and reference reports.. 1/RadCliQ-v1~\cite{yu2023evaluating.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: RadCliQ is a composite metric designed for evaluating radiology report generation, combining BLEU, BERTScore, SembScore, and RadGraph-F1 to provide a comprehensive assessment of generated reports.
elements.sent-2411-061: For our evaluation, we utilized the official implementation\footnote{\url{https://github.com/rajpurkarlab/CXR-Report-Metric , which also includes BLEU, BERTScore, SembScore, RadGraph-F1.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: While the original RadCliQ metric is designed as lower-is-better, we first calculate the average RadCliQ-v1 score for each model across the dataset, then take its reciprocal (1/RadCliQ-v1) to maintain consistency with other metrics where higher values indicate better performance.. RaTEScore~\cite{zhao2024ratescore.}
elements.sent-2411-063: RaTEScore is an entity-aware metric for radiology report evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-064: It emphasizes crucial medical entities like diagnostic outcomes and anatomical details and is robust against complex medical synonyms and sensitive to negation expressions.
elements.sent-2411-065: For our evaluation, we utilized the official implementation\footnote{\url{https://github.com/MAGIC-AI4Med/RaTEScore .. GREEN~\cite{ostmeier2024green.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: GREEN (Generative Radiology Report Evaluation and Error Notation) is an LLM-based metric for evaluating radiology report generation.
elements.sent-2411-067: It leverages language models to identify and explain clinically significant errors in quantitative and qualitative candidate reports.
elements.sent-2411-068: We utilized the official implementation\footnote{\url{https://github.com/Stanford-AIMI/GREEN for our evaluation.. 1/FineRadScore~\cite{huang2024fineradscore.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: FineRadScore is an automated evaluation metric leveraging an LLM to assess the quality of generated chest X-ray reports.
elements.sent-2411-070: It determines the minimum number of line-by-line corrections needed, with severity ratings from 1 to 4, to transform a candidate report into a ground-truth report.
elements.sent-2411-071: For evaluation, we employ GPT-4o as the LLM and use the official implementation\footnote{\url{https://github.com/rajpurkarlab/FineRadScore .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: To obtain a single FineRadScore for each report, we take the maximum clinical severity across all lines.
elements.sent-2411-073: Similar to 1/RadCliQ-v1, we present 1/FineRadScore to maintain consistency where higher values indicate better performance.
elements.sent-2411-074: In our analysis, we generate confidence intervals (CIs) by assuming a normal distribution of data.
elements.sent-2411-075: This statistical method calculates the mean and standard deviation of our data and then uses the standard error of the mean to estimate variability.
elements.sent-2411-076: For a 95\% confidence level, a Z-score of approximately 1.96 is used to determine the interval.
elements.sent-2411-077: This Z-score indicates that the true mean is likely within 1.96 standard errors of the sample mean.
elements.sent-2411-078: By multiplying the Z-score by the standard error, we obtain the CI, providing a range that encapsulates the true average value with 95\% certainty.
elements.sent-2411-079: BiomedGPT is a lightweight, open-source vision-language model designed for diverse biomedical tasks across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-080: The model was fine-tuned for VQA and image captioning tasks using multiple datasets, including radiology and pathology data.
elements.sent-2411-081: BiomedGPT\_IU is fine-tuned on the IU X-ray dataset for image captioning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-082: In our evaluation, we used the publicly available checkpoints trained on the IU-Xray dataset\footnote{\url{https://github.com/taokz/BiomedGPT .. CheXagent~\cite{chen2024chexagent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: CheXagent is an instruction-tuned foundation model specifically designed for chest X-ray interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-084: The model consists of a vision encoder for representing CXR images, and a network to bridge the vision and language modalities.
elements.sent-2411-085: This model is trained on CheXinstruct, a large-scale instruction-tuning dataset curated from 28 publicly-available datasets.
elements.sent-2411-086: For our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available 8 billion parameter checkpoint from Hugging Face\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/StanfordAIMI/CheXagent-8b .. CheXpertPlus\_CheX~\cite{chambon2024chexpert.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: CheXpertPlus\_CheX, introduced in the CheXpert Plus paper, utilizes a Swinv2~\cite{liu2022swin architecture with a two-layer BERT decoder~\cite{kenton2019bert for medical report generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: CheXpertPlus\_CheX is trained exclusively on the CheXpert Plus dataset.
elements.sent-2411-089: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available Findings Checkpoint\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/IAMJBchexpert-findings-baseline and Impression Checkpoint\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/IAMJB/chexpert-impression-baseline .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Our evaluation employs these models sequentially, generating the findings and impression sections separately, and then combining them with appropriate headers to form the complete report.. CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC~\cite{chambon2024chexpert.}
elements.sent-2411-091: CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC shares the same architectural design as CheXpertPlus\_MIMIC, employing the Swinv2 architecture with a two-layer BERT decoder.
elements.sent-2411-092: CheXpertPlus\_CheX is trained exclusively on the combination of MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert Plus dataset.
elements.sent-2411-093: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available Findings Checkpoint\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/IAMJB/chexpert-mimic-cxr-findings-baseline and Impression Checkpoint\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/IAMJB/chexpert-mimic-cxr-impression-baseline .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Our evaluation employs these models sequentially, generating the findings and impression sections separately, and then combining them with appropriate headers to form the complete report.. CheXpertPlus\_MIMIC~\cite{chambon2024chexpert.}
elements.sent-2411-095: CheXpertPlus\_MIMIC shares the same architectural design as CheXpertPlus\_CheX, employing the Swinv2 architecture with a two-layer BERT decoder.
elements.sent-2411-096: CheXpertPlus\_MIMIC comprises two distinct models trained on MIMIC-CXR: one for findings and another for impressions.
elements.sent-2411-097: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available Findings Checkpoint\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/IAMJB/mimic-cxr-findings-baseline and Impression Checkpoint\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/IAMJB/mimic-cxr-impression-baseline .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Our evaluation employs these models sequentially, generating the findings and impression sections separately, and then combining them with appropriate headers to form the complete report.
elements.sent-2411-099: Cvt2distilgpt2\_IU~\cite{nicolson2023improving CvT2DistilGPT2\_IU employs a hybrid architecture combining a Convolutional vision Transformer (CvT) encoder~\cite{wu2021cvt pre-trained on ImageNet-21K~\cite{deng2009imagenet with a DistilGPT2~\cite{alfarghaly2021automated decoder for chest X-ray report generation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: This model leverages the CvT's efficient hierarchical design for image feature extraction and DistilGPT2's natural language generation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-101: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available checkpoint from Github\footnote{\url{https://github.com/aehrc/cvt2distilgpt2 and followed the official evaluation guidelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Cvt2distilgpt2\_MIMIC~\cite{nicolson2023improving Cvt2distilgpt2\_MIMIC applies the same Cvt2distilgpt2 architecture but is trained on the MIMIC-CXR dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Our evaluation utilized the publicly available MIMIC-CXR-trained checkpoints.. RGRG~\cite{tanida2023interactive.}
elements.sent-2411-104: RGRG (Region-Guided Radiology Report Generation) employs object detection to extract localized visual features from 29 anatomical regions in chest X-rays.
elements.sent-2411-105: It uses binary classifiers to select salient features and encode abnormalities, followed by a language model generating sentences for each selected region.
elements.sent-2411-106: RGRG was trained on the Chest ImaGenome dataset~\cite{wu2021chest .}
elements.sent-2411-107: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available checkpoint from Github\footnote{\url{https://github.com/ttanida/rgrg and followed the official evaluation guidelines.. RaDialog~\cite{pellegrini2023radialog.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: RaDialog is a large vision-language model for radiology report generation and interactive dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-109: It integrates visual image features and structured pathology findings with a large language model (LLM), adapted to radiology using parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-110: RaDialog was trained on the MIMIC-CXR for radiology report generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-111: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available LLaVA version checkpoint from Huggingface\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/ChantalPellegrini/RaDialog-interactive-radiology-report-generation and followed the official evaluation guidelines.. GPT-4V~\cite{yang2023dawn.}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: GPT-4V (GPT-4 with vision) is a multimodal large language model released by OpenAI, which enables users to instruct GPT-4 to analyze image inputs provided by the user.
elements.sent-2411-113: In our evaluation, we used the API of model ``gpt4o05132024'' and followed the official evaluation protocols to assess its performance.
elements.sent-2411-114: The prompt we used is ``You are a helpful assistant.
elements.sent-2411-115: Please generate a report for the given images, including both findings and impressions.
elements.sent-2411-116: Return the report in the following format: Findings: \{\} Impression: \{\}.. ''.
elements.sent-2411-117: LLM-CXR is a multimodal large language model that utilizes VQ-GAN to tokenize images, integrating both image and text tokens as input to its base LLM architecture.
elements.sent-2411-118: This model enables CXR-to-report generation, report-to-CXR generation, and CXR-related visual question answering (VQA).
elements.sent-2411-119: For our evaluation, we used the publicly available checkpoints\footnote{\url{https://github.com/hyn2028/llm-cxr and followed the official evaluation guidelines.. MAIRA-2~\cite{bannur2024maira.}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: MAIRA-2 is a multimodal large language model that combines a radiology-specific image encoder with a Large Language Model (LLM), trained for grounded report generation from chest X-rays.
elements.sent-2411-121: For input, the model accepts X-ray images along with indication, comparison, and technique information.
elements.sent-2411-122: For our evaluation, we used the publicly available checkpoints\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/microsoft/maira-2 and followed the official evaluation guidelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: For studies containing both frontal and lateral views, we input the technique that ``PA and lateral views of the chest were obtained.''.
elements.sent-2411-124: For studies with only frontal views, we use ``PA view of the chest was obtained.''.. MedVersa~\cite{zhou2024generalist.}
elements.sent-2411-125: MedVersa is a versatile medical AI system that can coordinate multiple models and tools to perform various tasks and generate multimodal outputs.
elements.sent-2411-126: MedVersa was trained on the MIMIC-CXR training and validation dataset for medical report generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-127: In our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available checkpoint from Huggingface\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/hyzhou/MedVersa and followed the official evaluation guidelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The standard prompt structure we employed for report generation was ``Can you provide a report of \{input\_image\_token\} with findings and impression?'', where \{input\_image\_token\} represents the placeholder for the input image.. RadFM~\cite{wu2023towards.}
elements.sent-2411-129: RadFM is a radiology foundation model trained on large-scale multi-modal datasets.
elements.sent-2411-130: It supports both 2D and 3D scans, multi-image input, and visual-language interleaving cases.
elements.sent-2411-131: The model's training included the MIMIC-CXR dataset.
elements.sent-2411-132: For our evaluation, we utilized the publicly available checkpoint from Huggingface\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/chaoyi-wu/RadFM and followed the official evaluation guidelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The prompt we employed for report generation was ``Can you provide a radiology report for this medical image?''.. VLCI\_IU~\cite{chen2023cross.}
elements.sent-2411-134: VLCI~(Visual-Linguistic Causal Intervention) is a vision-language model using a multiway transformer for cross-modal alignment with Visual-linguistic causal intervention, integrating a pre-trained transformer and Visual and linguistic de-confounding Modules to mitigate cross-modal bias through local and global visual sampling and linguistic estimation using a vocabulary dictionary and visual features.
elements.sent-2411-135: In our evaluation, we used the publicly available checkpoints trained on the IU-Xray dataset\footnote{\url{https://github.com/WissingChen/VLCI .. VLCI\_MIMIC~\cite{chen2023cross.}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: VLCI\_MIMIC applies the same VLCI architecture but is trained on the MIMIC-CXR dataset.
elements.sent-2411-137: Our evaluation utilized the publicly available MIMIC-CXR-trained checkpoints.
elements.sent-2411-138: Table~\ref{tab:private_leaderboard ,~\ref{tab:mimiccxr_leaderboard ,~\ref{tab:iuxray_leaderboard and ~\ref{tab:chexpert_leaderboard summarize the performance of various medical report generation models across four different datasets: ReXGradient, MIMIC-CXR, IU X-ray and CheXpert Plus.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Among these, MedVersa demonstrates superior performance, achieving the best 1/RadCliQ-v1 scores on ReXGradient (1.01 짹 0.01), MIMIC-CXR (1.10 짹 0.02), and IU X-ray (1.46 짹 0.03) on the findings section.
elements.sent-2411-140: This consistent top performance across different datasets indicates MedVersa's robust generalization capabilities and effectiveness in medical report generation.
elements.sent-2411-141: Moreover, the results displayed in the tables show that ReXGradient serves as a dataset where models consistently exhibit minimal confidence intervals of 0.01 for most models on the RadCliQ-v1 metric, thereby supporting its utility as a reliable benchmark for medical report generation models.
elements.sent-2411-142: Figure~\ref{fig:metric_dataset illustrates the distribution of evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The IU X-ray dataset, while always showing high-performance scores (the best model achieving a 1/RadCliQ-v1 score of 1.46 짹 0.03 on the findings sections), suggests that it may be too simplistic or lacking in complexity necessary for rigorous model differentiation.
elements.sent-2411-144: In contrast, CheXpert Plus shows lower overall performance (the best model obtaining a 1/RadCliQ-v1 score of 0.81 짹 0.12 on the findings sections) with higher variance, potentially indicating dataset distribution shifts or noise.
elements.sent-2411-145: Models trained on multiple datasets (e.g., CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC) tend to outperform those trained on individual datasets, suggesting that a multi-dataset training approach helps bridge the distributional gap and enhances generalization.
elements.sent-2411-146: Models perform better when evaluated on the same distribution seen in training, for instance, VLCI\_IU achieves superior performance on IU X-ray (RadCliQ-v1: 1.38 짹 0.04) compared to VLCI\_MIMIC (0.91 짹 0.04), while VLCI\_MIMIC performs better on MIMIC-CXR (0.68 짹 0.02 vs 0.60 짹 0.02 for VLCI\_IU).
elements.sent-2411-147: The comparison between findings-only and findings + impression tasks reveals interesting model behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-148: On ReXGradient, MedVersa shows slight performance degradation when generating both findings and impressions (1/RadCliQ-v1 decreasing from 1.01 짹 0.01 to 0.98 짹 0.05), while CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC shows improvement (from 0.83 짹 0.01 to 0.85 짹 0.01).
elements.sent-2411-149: This may be due to CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC using separate models for findings and impression generation, while MedVersa only uses a single model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-150: The separate models may allow CheXpertPlus\_CheX\_MIMIC to better specialize in each subtask.
